exemplar.title: How do illuminance of incident light and surrounding temperature affect the efficiency of photovoltaic cells?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do illuminance of incident light and surrounding temperature affect the efficiency of photovoltaic cells?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Consistently clear and effective communication of the topic and its purpose throughout the report.
Research question is precisely formulated, tightly focused, and referenced at key stages of hypothesis, methodology, and analysis.
weaknesses: Introduction includes an engaging but digressive anecdote that slightly detracts from concision.
Methodology has minor completeness issues (e.g., uncontrolled bulb change and incomplete control-variable table entries).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs investigation demonstrates strong focus and method: the topic is communicated accurately and consistently (5â6 band), and the research question is clear and tightly focused (5â6 band). Although the methodology is generally appropriate and shows evidence of informed selection, a few uncontrolled variables and incomplete table entries limit full completeness, placing this work at the top of the 3â4 band for that strand. Balancing these factors yields a final criterion score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Good application of core physics concepts (photoelectric effect, efficiency relations) with competent use of analytical tools (regression, Pearsonâs r).
Terminology such as illuminance, luminous efficacy, and statistical symbols are used appropriately in most instances, and figures are well labelled.
weaknesses: Occasional inconsistencies in terminology and unit notation (e.g., lm Wâ»Â¹ vs lm/W and misrendered Ï symbol).
Theoretical treatments are descriptive rather than deeply analytical (e.g., no formal inverse-square derivation, missing derivation of efficiency formula).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate shows solid subject knowledge and understanding: relevant physics is applied accurately and sources are integrated coherently, placing the work in the 3â4 band for subject knowledge. Use of terminology is mostly correct but not flawlessly consistent, aligning with the 3â4 band for concepts. Combining these assessments supports a final score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Analysis is clearly tied to the research question, with logical regression and correlation interpretation.
Arguments are well-structured, with coherent discussion of data trends and acknowledgement of anomalies (e.g., the 5792 lx point).
weaknesses: The argument remains largely descriptive with limited exploration of alternative explanations (e.g., series resistance or spectral effects).
Critical evaluation of errors and uncertainties is present but lacks quantitative depth and broader methodological alternatives (e.g., solar simulator usage).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall critical thinking performance sits solidly within the 4â6 band: research analysis is relevant and competent; discussion is coherent; and evaluation shows emerging but not exhaustive critical perspectives. To reflect the studentâs strengths in tying data to the question and structuring arguments, while acknowledging areas for deeper evaluation, this criterion is awarded 6 out of 12.

strengths: Report structure follows conventions rigorously, with title page, TOC, section headings, and sequential page numbers.
Visual elements (tables, figures) are generally well integrated and captioned, supporting reader comprehension.
weaknesses: Minor formatting inconsistencies such as table misalignments and inconsistent indentation in the TOC.
Some low-resolution images and occasional font-size variations slightly detract from visual polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation quality clearly meets the descriptors for the 3â4 band: the structure is coherent and conventions are followed, and visuals support the narrative effectively. Despite a few aesthetic and formatting inconsistencies, the overall presentation is strong, justifying the maximum score of 4 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Specialist terms such as illuminance, luminous flux, luminous efficacy, Pearsonâs r and p-value are used correctly in most instances, and equations are generally well defined. Figures are labelled with units and axes, supporting reader comprehension.  

Yet there are recurrent inconsistenciesâe.g., interchangeable use of âluminous efficacyâ and âluminous flux,â mixed unit formats, and the incorrect statement about rejecting the alternate hypothesis. These lapses indicate that terminology, while largely accurate, is not flawlessly consistent. This corresponds to the 3-4 band; 4 marks are awarded.

The discussion proceeds logically: temperature is isolated first to identify an optimal value, followed by an examination of illuminance. Claims are consistently tied back to data, and strengths/weaknesses of results are acknowledged (e.g., possible anomaly at 5792 lx).  

However, the argument remains essentially descriptive; alternative explanations (such as series resistance or spectral mismatch) are not explored, and the broader significance of findings is only briefly mentioned. Some mis-statement of statistical inference also impedes sophistication. These features indicate a well-developed but not highly sophisticated argument, meriting 5 marks within the 4-6 band.

The student provides a detailed list of apparatus with uncertainties, a control-variable table, preliminary-trial reflections and a step-by-step procedure, indicating a generally appropriate and informed methodological choice.  

However, several limitations reduce completeness: the change from a 100 W to a 40 W bulb introduces an uncontrolled variable; uncertainty propagation is demonstrated for only one data point; and the decision not to model distanceâilluminance formally (e.g., inverse-square law) restricts methodological rigour. Consequently, the work sits comfortably in the 3-4 band and is awarded the upper mark of 4.

The essay applies relevant physicsâphotoelectric effect, power and efficiency relations, temperature dependence of semiconductor behaviourâwith generally correct explanations and appropriate use of equations. Data handling (e.g., Pearsonâs r, p-value, linear regression) demonstrates competent analytical knowledge.  

Nevertheless, theoretical treatment is mostly descriptive; deeper engagement with semiconductor band-gap physics or more advanced modelling of non-linear lightâefficiency relationships is absent. Occasional confusion between luminous efficacy and luminous flux and inconsistent unit notation (lm Wâ»Â¹ vs lm/W) reveal gaps in mastery. Overall, the work reflects solid but not exemplary subject understanding, placing it in the 3-4 band at 4 marks.

The evaluation section identifies several limitationsâbulb geometry, sample size, instrument calibrationâand proposes practical improvements (use of diffuser, more trials, recalibration). This shows an emerging critical perspective.  

Yet the evaluation is somewhat detached from the main analysis, lacks depth on how errors quantitatively influence results, and offers limited consideration of broader theoretical or methodological alternatives (e.g., using a solar simulator). Consequently the work aligns with the lower end of the 4-6 band for critical evaluation; 4 marks are assigned.

The student states the topic and its real-world relevance in the opening section, then restates it succinctly in the title, TOC and throughout the text. Links between photovoltaic efficiency, illuminance and temperature are introduced early and revisited in analysis and conclusion, giving the work a clear sense of purpose.  

Minor weaknessesâsuch as occasional digressions in the anecdotal introduction and some redundancy in later sectionsâslightly reduce concision, but overall the communication of the topic and its intent remains consistently accurate and effective. Hence the work fits best in the 5-6 band and is awarded 5 marks within that band.

The document uses a clean, logical layout: tables are bordered and captioned, figures are labelled, and error bars are included. Visual elements (graphs, apparatus diagram) clearly support the narrative despite some low-resolution images and slight misplacement of a few labels.  

Text and visuals are generally well balanced, enhancing readability. Minor aesthetic flaws (pixelated images, inconsistent font sizes) are present but do not hinder communication. These features satisfy the 3-4 band, and 4 marks are awarded.

The research question, âHow do illuminance of incident light and surrounding temperature affect the efficiency of photovoltaic cells?â, is clearly formulated, tightly focused on two variables and used as a reference point in hypothesis, data processing and conclusion.  

Although the null and alternate hypotheses contain a slip (rejecting the alternate rather than the null) and temperature is omitted from the null, these flaws do not undermine the basic clarity or focus of the RQ. The link between question and subsequent discussion remains strong; therefore the strand aligns with the 5-6 descriptor and merits 5 marks.

The student performs linear regressions, calculates Pearsonâs correlation coefficients and p-values, and interprets slopes quantitatively (e.g., â0.149 % Â°Câ»Â¹). Analysis is clearly connected to the RQ and hypotheses, showing a good level of competence.  

Nonetheless, modelling is restricted to simple linear fits despite evidence of potential non-linear behaviour at high illuminance, and uncertainty analysis is limited to a single sample calculation. Comparative discussion with literature values of PV efficiency is missing. Overall the work represents a solid, though not exemplary, analytical treatment, fitting mid-range of the 4-6 band; 5 marks are appropriate.

The essay contains a title page with word count, an organised table of contents, section headings following EE conventions, sequential page numbers and appropriate citation section. Figures and tables are generally well integrated with captions, and mathematical expressions are typeset clearly.  

Minor issuesâoccasional misaligned tables, sporadic wrapping problems and small formatting inconsistenciesâprevent a flawless presentation, but the overall structure is coherent and facilitates understanding. This clearly meets the descriptors for the 3-4 band, earning 4 marks.

annotations.comment: Uncertainty propagation is demonstrated for only one sample. To evaluate overall reliability, apply the full propagation method across representative data points.
The raw data table at 10 cm lists only two trials. Include the third measurement to uphold consistency and enable valid averaging.
The anecdotal introduction on astronomy, while engaging, digresses from the core focus. Consider streamlining this section to maintain direct relevance to photovoltaic efficiency.
The word count is provided at the start, satisfying the formatting requirement and demonstrating adherence to Extended Essay administrative conventions.
Indentation of sub-entries in the table of contents is inconsistent, which may confuse the hierarchy of topics. Standardize spacing to improve visual coherence.
Relying solely on the manufacturerâs label for luminous flux (11 lm/W) introduces uncertainty. Consider measuring or citing a primary source to validate this value.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well integrated, facilitating easy navigation through all major sections and sub-sections of the report.

The topic statement is concise and well positioned, clearly conveying the investigationâs focus on solar panel efficiency under varying conditions. This immediate clarity establishes a strong sense of purpose and context for the reader.

The research question is precisely and prominently stated, defining the independent variables and the target phenomenon clearly. This focused formulation guides the subsequent hypothesis, methodology and analysis effectively.
The symbol for luminous flux (Ï) is rendered as âââ inconsistently. Use the standard Greek letter \(\phi\) and define it clearly to avoid confusion.
The rationale linking childhood interest to the research question is clear and effectively justifies the investigationâs relevance to optimizing solar energy conversion.
The background explains illuminance qualitatively but omits a formal derivation using the inverseâsquare law. Including that model would strengthen the theoretical foundation.
Including nonessential items (e.g., a phone) in the materials list dilutes focus. Retain only apparatus directly related to measurements to enhance methodological clarity.
The apparatus tableâs ruler uncertainty conversion (Â±0.5 cm = Â±0.005 m) is correct but should include how repeated measurements reduce random error. Address sampling strategy here.
The equation \(P=\phi/\eta\) is presented without derivation. Explain the origin of this relationship and its assumptions for full conceptual clarity.
The description of luminous efficacy conflates photometric and conversion efficiency concepts. Clarify that luminous efficacy refers to the sourceâs lumen output per watt, not PV cell performance.

Procedure step 2 uses the 100 W bulb for temperature control then switches to 40 W for illuminance, altering thermal conditions. This sequence may bias results; revise to maintain consistent thermal regime.
The efficiency formula uses a percentage multiplier without justification. Explicitly state why the ratio is multiplied by 100\% and how it integrates into the PV context.
The controlâvariable table header is present but lacks entries detailing how each variable is held constant. Complete the table to demonstrate methodological rigor.
Procedure describes distances to 20 cm, yet data include 25 cm. Ensure procedural steps match data collection or explain the extension in the methodology.
Table captions such as âTemperature had no effects on illuminanceâ are ambiguous. Rephrase to clarify that illuminance was measured independently of temperature variations.

The null and alternate hypotheses omit the temperature variable and misstate which hypothesis to reject. Reformulate to include both illuminance and temperature accurately and correctly assign null/alternate roles.
Switching between a 100 W and 40 W bulb introduces an uncontrolled variable in light source spectral output. Standardize the source or justify its impact analytically.
The temperature-efficiency graph is low resolution and lacks error bars. Improve image quality and include uncertainty indicators to bolster data interpretation.
The p-value interpretation statement incorrectly concludes rejection of the alternate hypothesis. It should state rejection of the null, then accept the alternate.
The processed data table presents intercept at 0 lx efficiency but lacks discussion of physical meaning. Contextualize this value or adjust axes to origin for realistic interpretation.
Inline equation âefficiency = â0.149(temperature)+24.94â is not formatted in LaTeX like other expressions; standardize all mathematical notation for consistency.
The reported input power (0.121 W) in uncertainty steps is inconsistent with the earlier calculated 6.99 W. Verify and correct this to ensure accurate uncertainty analysis.
When discussing Pearsonâs correlation, use the conventional symbol ârâ rather than uppercase âR.â Consistent terminology improves statistical clarity.
Evaluation strengths are listed but not linked back to the research questionâs aims. Explicitly connect these strengths to how they reinforce the investigationâs focus.
Only one methodological improvement is proposed. Expand this section to include theoretical extensions (e.g., spectral distribution) and alternative measurement techniques.
Bibliography entries use mixed styles for titles and URLs. Adopt a consistent citation format (e.g., MLA or APA) and apply it uniformly.
